content.text: Scaling laws are empirical power-law relationships that describe how a language model's test loss decreases as its size, training data, or compute budget increases. Understanding these relationships lets practitioners allocate compute budgets rationally, predict the performance of a model before training it, and avoid systematic errors like training a model that is too large for its data budget or too small for its compute budget.
What Scaling Laws Are
A scaling law is a mathematical relationship of the form

where L is the model's test loss and X is a resource variable such as the number of model parameters N, the number of training tokens D, or the total compute in FLOPs C. On a log-log plot, these relationships appear as straight lines, making them easy to fit and extrapolate.

Two landmark papers define modern scaling law thinking. Kaplan et al. (2020) from OpenAI established that loss scales predictably with N and C over many orders of magnitude, and concluded that under a fixed compute budget, one should prioritise larger models trained on fewer tokens. Hoffmann et al. (2022) from DeepMind (the Chinchilla paper) corrected this: they showed that model size N and training tokens D should be scaled equally, roughly satisfying
for compute-optimal training. This correction had major practical consequences: GPT-3 style models were massively under-trained relative to their parameter count.
Why Scaling Laws Matter

Training a frontier language model costs tens of millions of dollars and takes weeks on thousands of GPUs. A mismatch between model size and training tokens does not just waste compute — it produces a model that is more expensive to serve (too large) or underperforms its compute budget (too small). Scaling laws convert these otherwise opaque decisions into engineering calculations with quantifiable trade-offs.

Beyond compute allocation, scaling laws provide a predictive tool: by running small ablations at 1 percent of the planned compute budget, practitioners can extrapolate expected loss at full scale. This proxy evaluation drives decisions about architecture, tokeniser, and data mixture long before the full training run begins. The Chinchilla scaling laws are the reason that LLaMA-2 7B was trained on 2T tokens (approximately 286 tokens per parameter) rather than the 300B tokens (approximately 43 tokens per parameter) used for GPT-3 175B.
Benefits

Predictable performance: loss at full scale can be estimated from small-scale ablations with a margin of error of a few percent on log-loss, enabling rational planning before committing the full compute budget.

Compute-optimal training: Chinchilla scaling directly tells practitioners the optimal (N, D) pair for any compute budget C, eliminating the intuition-driven over-sizing that characterised pre-2022 models.
Architecture comparisons: by holding compute constant and comparing loss curves across architecture variants, teams can make fair, cost-controlled architectural decisions.

Data quality signal: deviations from the expected scaling curve at a given token count indicate data quality problems — noisy or redundant data flattens the loss curve below the expected power-law slope.
Hardware planning: scaling laws connect loss targets to compute budgets and thus to GPU-hours, enabling cost estimates for models that have not been built yet.
Trade-offs and Limitations
Loss vs. capability: scaling laws predict cross-entropy loss, not task performance. Emergent capabilities appear suddenly at scale and are not predicted by smooth power laws.

Domain specificity: scaling laws fitted on a general web corpus do not transfer directly to domain-specific corpora such as code, math, or biomedical text. The exponents differ across data distributions.
Architecture dependence: the Chinchilla exponents apply to standard dense Transformer models. Mixture-of-experts architectures and state space models have different scaling exponents.

Post-training effects: RLHF, DPO, and instruction tuning can substantially improve a model's effective performance independent of its pretraining loss, decoupling downstream task quality from the loss metric the scaling law predicts.
Data saturation: when training tokens approach the size of the available corpus and repetition begins, the loss curve no longer follows the expected power law.
How Scaling Laws Work
The key insight is that model loss as a function of each resource variable is well approximated by a power law over many orders of magnitude. The individual and joint laws are described below.
The Kaplan et al. (2020) Laws

The original OpenAI scaling laws measured how loss varies with parameters, tokens, and compute independently, holding the other two large. With enough parameters (fully converged on all available data), loss follows a power law in parameter count:
With enough data but a finite model size, loss follows a separate power law in token count:
Kaplan et al. concluded that under a fixed compute budget, model size N matters more than training duration D, because
— loss improves faster by adding parameters than by adding tokens. This analysis led to the design of GPT-3: 175B parameters trained on only 300B tokens.
The Chinchilla Correction (Hoffmann et al., 2022)

Hoffmann et al. challenged the Kaplan conclusion by training over 400 models ranging from 70M to 16B parameters on 5B to 400B tokens. They found that compute-optimal training requires jointly optimising N and D. Using the FLOPs approximation
for Transformer training, the compute-optimal allocation satisfies:

attrs.latex: N_{\text{opt}}(C) = G_N \cdot C^{0.5}, \quad D_{\text{opt}}(C) = G_D \cdot C^{0.5}

content.text: where
and
. Dividing gives

: a compute-optimal model should be trained on approximately 20 tokens per parameter. The model validating this rule — Chinchilla at 70B parameters trained on 1.4T tokens — outperformed Gopher at 280B parameters despite using four times fewer parameters and the same compute budget.
The Full Scaling Law with Irreducible Entropy
The joint scaling law combines parameter and data scaling into a single expression that includes an irreducible entropy floor
— the minimum possible loss on a given data distribution regardless of model size or training length:

Fitting this form requires measuring loss at many (N, D) combinations and optimising for the parameters A, alpha, B, beta, and L_inf. The irreducible term captures the inherent entropy of natural language — the ambiguity and stochasticity that no model can eliminate regardless of scale.
Architecture

The diagram below shows two complementary views of scaling laws. The left panel shows how compute-optimal (Chinchilla) training achieves lower loss than under-sized models at the same compute budget. The right panel shows the Chinchilla optimal frontier: as parameters N increase, training tokens D must increase proportionally (D = 20N), with actual model checkpoints (1B/20B tokens, 7B/140B tokens, 70B/1.4T tokens) lying on this line.

attrs.alt: Scaling laws: left chart shows compute vs test loss with Chinchilla-optimal achieving lower loss than under-sized models at same compute. Right chart shows optimal N vs D frontier with model checkpoints.

content.text: When to Use Scaling Laws

Scaling laws are most valuable when the compute budget is large enough that the choice of N and D meaningfully affects outcome, typically above 10^26 FLOPs. Below this threshold, the compute difference between Chinchilla-optimal and sub-optimal allocation is small in absolute terms.
Use scaling laws to plan training runs when the compute budget exceeds 10^23 FLOPs (roughly one A100 GPU-year).
Use scaling laws to compare architectures at the same compute budget, not at the same parameter count.
Use scaling laws to detect data quality problems: if the observed loss is above the expected scaling curve at a given compute, data quality may be the bottleneck.

Do not use scaling laws alone to decide when to stop training for a model intended for fine-tuning: a model trained beyond compute-optimal receives extra tokens at diminishing returns, but the resulting model may be smaller and cheaper to serve.
Do not rely on published exponents for domain-specific models such as medical, legal, or code models: refit the exponents on a small grid of ablations in your target domain.
How to Apply Scaling Laws

Applying scaling laws in practice requires a small-scale ablation grid, loss measurement at each (N, D) point, and a curve-fitting step to estimate the scaling exponents for the specific data distribution and architecture.
Define the compute budget: determine the total FLOPs available using C = 6ND for standard Transformer training.
Run a 3x3 ablation grid: train models at different (N, D) combinations, each at 1 to 5 percent of the target compute, measuring validation loss at end of training.
Fit the scaling law: use least-squares optimisation to fit L(N, D) = A/N^alpha + B/D^beta + L_inf to the ablation results.
Compute the optimal allocation: substitute the full compute budget C into the formulas for N_opt and D_opt.
Verify at intermediate scale: run one model at 10 percent of the full budget at the predicted optimal (N, D) and check that the observed loss matches the prediction.

"""Chinchilla optimal compute allocator.

Given a compute budget in FLOPs, returns the compute-optimal
number of parameters and training tokens using the Chinchilla
coefficients from Hoffmann et al. (2022).
"""
import math


def chinchilla_optimal(compute_flops: float) -> tuple[float, float]:
    """
    Returns (N_opt, D_opt) for the given compute budget.

    Uses Hoffmann et al. (2022) Approach 3 coefficients:
      N_opt = G_N * C^0.5
      D_opt = G_D * C^0.5
    where G_N is approximately 0.00395.

    Note: C = 6 * N * D for a standard Transformer forward + backward pass.
    """
    G_N = 0.00395
    G_D = 1.0 / G_N
    N_opt = G_N * (compute_flops ** 0.5)
    D_opt = G_D * (compute_flops ** 0.5)
    return N_opt, D_opt


def verify_flop_budget(N: float, D: float) -> float:
    """Compute the FLOPs implied by N parameters and D training tokens."""
    return 6 * N * D


if __name__ == "__main__":
    budgets = {
        "1B GPU-hours (~H100)": 3.12e20,
        "10B GPU-hours": 3.12e21,
        "100B GPU-hours (frontier)": 3.12e22,
    }
    for label, C in budgets.items():
        N_opt, D_opt = chinchilla_optimal(C)
        print(f"{label}:")
        print(f"  N_opt  = {N_opt/1e9:.2f}B parameters")
        print(f"  D_opt  = {D_opt/1e9:.0f}B tokens")
        print(f"  D/N    = {D_opt/N_opt:.1f} tokens per parameter")
        implied = verify_flop_budget(N_opt, D_opt)
        print(f"  FLOPs  = {implied:.2e} (budget: {C:.2e})")
        print()
Examples
Fitting a Power Law to Ablation Data
Given a set of (N, D, loss) triples from a small ablation grid, the following code fits the parametric scaling law to the data using scipy's nonlinear least-squares optimiser.

import numpy as np
from scipy.optimize import curve_fit

# Ablation results: (N_params, D_tokens, val_loss)
# Run these 9 models at approximately 1% of your target compute budget.
ablation_data = [
    (70e6,  1.4e9,  3.15),
    (70e6,  7e9,   2.96),
    (70e6,  35e9,  2.82),
    (400e6, 8e9,   2.89),
    (400e6, 40e9,  2.71),
    (400e6, 200e9, 2.58),
    (1.5e9, 30e9,  2.78),
    (1.5e9, 150e9, 2.59),
    (1.5e9, 750e9, 2.46),
]

N_arr = np.array([d[0] for d in ablation_data])
D_arr = np.array([d[1] for d in ablation_data])
L_arr = np.array([d[2] for d in ablation_data])


def scaling_law(ND, log_A, alpha, log_B, beta, L_inf):
    """Joint scaling law: L(N, D) = A/N^alpha + B/D^beta + L_inf."""
    N, D = ND
    A = np.exp(log_A)
    B = np.exp(log_B)
    return A / (N ** alpha) + B / (D ** beta) + L_inf


# Initial parameter guess
p0 = [np.log(1e9), 0.35, np.log(1e9), 0.35, 1.8]

popt, _ = curve_fit(
    scaling_law,
    (N_arr, D_arr),
    L_arr,
    p0=p0,
    maxfev=10000,
)

log_A, alpha, log_B, beta, L_inf = popt
print("Fitted parameters:")
print(f"  A     = {np.exp(log_A):.3e}")
print(f"  alpha = {alpha:.4f}")
print(f"  B     = {np.exp(log_B):.3e}")
print(f"  beta  = {beta:.4f}")
print(f"  L_inf = {L_inf:.4f}")

# Predict loss at full scale: 7B params, 140B tokens
N_full, D_full = 7e9, 140e9
L_pred = scaling_law((N_full, D_full), *popt)
print(f"\nPredicted loss at 7B / 140B tokens: {L_pred:.4f}")
Compute-Optimal Training Plan

"""Generate a compute-optimal training run configuration from a GPU cluster spec."""
from dataclasses import dataclass


@dataclass
class ClusterSpec:
    n_gpus: int
    flops_per_gpu_per_sec: float  # bfloat16 FLOP/s (e.g. 312e12 for H100 SXM)
    utilisation: float = 0.35     # practical Model FLOP Utilisation (MFU)


@dataclass
class TrainConfig:
    n_params_B: float
    n_tokens_B: float
    compute_days: float
    tokens_per_param: float


def plan_training_run(cluster: ClusterSpec, budget_days: float) -> TrainConfig:
    """Return a Chinchilla-optimal training config for a given GPU-day budget."""
    flops_per_day = (
        cluster.n_gpus
        * cluster.flops_per_gpu_per_sec
        * cluster.utilisation
        * 86_400
    )
    total_flops = flops_per_day * budget_days

    G_N = 0.00395
    N_opt = G_N * total_flops ** 0.5
    D_opt = total_flops / (6 * N_opt)  # from C = 6ND

    return TrainConfig(
        n_params_B=N_opt / 1e9,
        n_tokens_B=D_opt / 1e9,
        compute_days=budget_days,
        tokens_per_param=D_opt / N_opt,
    )


# Example: 256 H100 SXM GPUs for 30 days
cluster = ClusterSpec(n_gpus=256, flops_per_gpu_per_sec=312e12)
config = plan_training_run(cluster, budget_days=30)
print(f"Chinchilla-optimal for 256 x H100 x 30 days:")
print(f"  Model size : {config.n_params_B:.1f}B parameters")
print(f"  Tokens     : {config.n_tokens_B:.0f}B tokens")
print(f"  D/N ratio  : {config.tokens_per_param:.1f}")
Real-World Example

The LLaMA series demonstrates the practical impact of Chinchilla scaling. LLaMA 1 (Touvron et al., 2023) trained a 7B-parameter model on 1T tokens — roughly 143 tokens per parameter, seven times the Chinchilla ratio — not because the authors were unaware of the Chinchilla rule, but because they were optimising for inference efficiency. A smaller, more extensively trained model is cheaper to serve at the same quality level than a larger, less-trained model. LLaMA 1 7B outperformed GPT-3 175B on many benchmarks despite having 25 times fewer parameters, directly demonstrating the cost of under-training the larger model.

LLaMA 3 (2024) pushed this further: the 8B model was trained on 15T tokens (approximately 1875 tokens per parameter), far beyond the Chinchilla-optimal amount. This produced a model that rivals others several times larger trained at the Chinchilla ratio, confirming that for deployment-oriented training, significantly exceeding compute-optimal token counts yields continuing improvements at the cost of longer training runs — a trade-off that becomes attractive when the inference workload is large enough to justify the training investment.
Common Mistakes
Applying Chinchilla exponents to domain-specific corpora: the 20-token rule was fitted on a mix of web text. For code or math, the optimal D/N ratio differs; refit on your data.

Confusing compute-optimal with inference-optimal: Chinchilla minimises loss for a given training compute. If serving cost matters more than training cost, training a smaller model for more tokens often wins.

Ignoring the FLOPs constant: C = 6ND is an approximation for standard Transformer training. Different architectures have different FLOPs multipliers and the Chinchilla formulas do not transfer directly.
Extrapolating too far: scaling law extrapolation beyond 10x the largest ablation point has high uncertainty; the power-law slope can change at very large scale.
Treating loss as a proxy for all capabilities: emergent abilities like in-context learning appear non-linearly with scale and are not predicted by the smooth loss curve.
Using final loss instead of per-epoch training curves: scaling laws should be fitted to losses measured at multiple checkpoints during training, not just the final loss.
Best Practices
Run ablations at 0.1%, 1%, and 5% of the target compute budget on a grid spanning at least one order of magnitude in N and D independently.
Fit the scaling law using per-token loss measured on a fixed held-out validation set, not the training loss, to avoid confounds from learning rate schedule effects.
Report the fitted exponents (alpha, beta) alongside the scaling curves; they carry more information than the curve fit alone and reveal architecture differences.

Use the scaling law for architecture comparison at equal compute, not equal parameter count; a model with more attention heads has higher FLOPs per token and is not cheaper to train at the same parameter count.

When the intended use is instruction tuning or RLHF, consider training beyond compute-optimal (more tokens, smaller model) to reduce inference cost at the quality level the alignment pipeline targets.
Performance and Scalability

Scaling laws themselves are computationally cheap to fit — the ablation grid is the expensive part, not the curve fitting. A well-designed ablation grid covering a 3x3 matrix of (N, D) values at 1% of the target compute costs roughly 9% of the full training run in compute, which is typically acceptable for training runs above 10^24 FLOPs.

Beyond the original dense-Transformer scaling laws, recent work shows that mixture-of-experts (MoE) models have different effective scaling exponents. An MoE model with k active experts and E total experts has approximately E/k times as many parameters as a dense model at the same compute cost. Empirically, MoE models scale more favourably with parameter count than dense models, meaning the optimal D/N ratio shifts: MoE models tend to be larger relative to their compute budget compared to compute-optimal dense models. The published Chinchilla exponents should not be applied directly to MoE architectures without refitting.
Tooling and Ecosystem
Scaling law work relies on general-purpose ML infrastructure rather than specialised tools, reflecting the experiment-driven nature of the field.
PyTorch with Megatron-LM or FSDP: the standard stack for large-scale ablation training; both provide tensor and pipeline parallelism needed for multi-billion-parameter ablations.
LitGPT: lightweight, hackable GPT training codebase well-suited for small-scale scaling ablations; easy to modify for custom data mixtures and architecture variants.
scipy.optimize.curve_fit: the standard tool for fitting the parametric scaling law to ablation data, as demonstrated in the examples above.
Weights and Biases: essential for tracking loss curves across the ablation grid, comparing loss at equal compute across architectures, and plotting scaling curves in a shareable format.
EleutherAI scaling-book: open-source replication of the Chinchilla scaling analysis with detailed methodology; useful as a reference implementation for fitting scaling laws on custom data.
Comparison with Alternatives

Security and Reliability
Scaling laws are empirical, not theoretical guarantees. Relying on scaling law predictions without verification at intermediate scale introduces production risk.
Distribution shift in ablation data: if the ablation models use a different tokeniser, context length, or data mixture than the full run, the fitted exponents will not transfer.
Overfitting in the curve fit: with fewer than 9 ablation points, the six-parameter joint scaling law is underdetermined and may extrapolate poorly.
Phase transitions: emergent abilities appear as discontinuities in downstream task performance that are invisible in the smooth loss curve; always evaluate key downstream benchmarks alongside loss.
Mental Model

Think of training a language model like filling a tank with knowledge, where the tank capacity is N (parameters) and the water supply is D (training tokens). A small tank fills quickly and overflows — analogous to a small model that memorises but cannot generalise. A large tank takes a very long time to fill — analogous to a large model that needs far more tokens to reach its capacity. Chinchilla scaling says: for any given water supply (compute budget), the optimal tank size is where the marginal cost of a larger tank exactly equals the marginal benefit of more capacity. That balance point is D divided by N equals approximately 20.
The irreducible entropy floor

is the minimum loss corresponding to the inherent unpredictability of language — the uncertainty that no model, however large and well-trained, can eliminate. Just as a perfect weather model cannot eliminate forecast uncertainty due to chaotic dynamics, a perfect language model cannot predict the next word in a genuinely ambiguous sentence. Recognising this floor prevents the error of expecting loss to keep decreasing indefinitely: at some point, the remaining loss is irreducible and additional compute provides zero further benefit on the training distribution.
Key Takeaways
Scaling laws are power-law relationships predicting model loss as a function of parameters N, tokens D, or compute C over many orders of magnitude.
The Chinchilla correction (Hoffmann et al., 2022) showed that N and D should scale equally: the compute-optimal ratio is approximately D = 20N tokens per parameter.
The compute budget C = 6ND drives the optimal allocation: N_opt is proportional to the square root of C, and D_opt = C / (6 * N_opt).
The full scaling law L(N, D) = A/N^alpha + B/D^beta + L_inf includes an irreducible entropy floor that no amount of scale can reduce.
Scaling laws predict cross-entropy loss, not emergent capabilities; abilities like in-context learning can appear non-linearly and are not predicted by smooth power-law extrapolation.
For deployment-oriented training, exceeding compute-optimal token counts (smaller model, more tokens) reduces inference cost without major quality degradation, as shown by the LLaMA series.
Fitting your own scaling exponents on a small ablation grid is the most reliable way to apply these principles to non-standard architectures or domain-specific corpora.
